title: Interpretable deep learning strategy for accurate hierarchical RNA-protein interaction prediction
author: Liu, Haoquan
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: RNA-protein complexes are essential for many biological processes, including fundamental translation and gene regulation. However, computational prediction of RNA-protein structures with unknown templates and limited multiple sequence alignments remains challenging, often resulting in discrepancies between the predicted interfaces and those observed experimentally. Accurate prediction of RNA binding sites can shed light on the overall framework for precise complex prediction. Here, we introduce ZHMolSite, a general framework that uses a hierarchical learning strategy to identify protein-binding nucleotides on RNA molecules. ZHMolSite outperforms AlphaFold3, RoseTTAFold2NA, and RoseTTAFold-All-Atom,   showing improvements of 4.5%-13.2% and 9.2%-22.5% in identifying RNA-binding sites at the interface for the TE and CASP_PZ sets. It also achieves enhancements of 7.5%-25.3% and 13.9%-21.3% in predicting RNA-protein complex structures using interacting-nucleotide information, all without dependence   on templates or MSAs.   We believe the approach behind ZHMolSite will contribute to solving the ongoing challenge of accurately predicting RNA-protein structures.

However, computational prediction of RNA-protein structures with unknown templates and limited multiple sequence alignments remains challenging, often resulting in discrepancies between the predicted interfaces and those observed experimentally. Accurate prediction of RNA binding sites can shed light on the overall framework for precise complex prediction. It also achieves enhancements of 7.5%-25.3% and 13.9%-21.3% in predicting RNA-protein complex structures using interacting-nucleotide information, all without dependence   on templates or MSAs.   We believe the approach behind ZHMolSite will contribute to solving the ongoing challenge of accurately predicting RNA-protein structures.
